family_name: General Surgery — Staple removal surgical follow-up
population: People with relevant surgical staple removal follow-up history, findings and clinician decisions entered.
intended_setting: Partial for wound review; staple-removal timing, technique and UAE procedural requirements remain unsupported.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: People have the right to be involved in discussions and make informed decisions about their care, as described in NICE's information on making decisions about your care . Making decisions using NICE guidelines explains how we use words to show the strength (or certainty) of our recommendations, and has information about prescribing medicines (including off-label use), professional guidelines, standards and laws (including on consent and mental capacity), and safeguarding.

heading_path: Surgical site infections: prevention and treatment

evidence_statements.population: Adults, young people, and children having a surgical procedure involving a skin incision.
evidence_statements.setting: Perioperative, postoperative, community, and specialist wound-care services.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Offer patients and carers clear, consistent information and advice throughout all stages of their care. This should include the risks of surgical site infections , what is being done to reduce them and how they are managed. For more guidance on providing information to adults and discussing their preferences with them, see the NICE guideline on patient experience in adult NHS services . [2008]
evidence_statements.source_title: Surgical site infections: prevention and treatment — Recommendations

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Offer patients and carers information and advice on how to care for their wound after discharge. [2008]

heading_path: Surgical site infections: prevention and treatment

evidence_statements.population: Adults, young people, and children having a surgical procedure involving a skin incision.
evidence_statements.setting: Perioperative, postoperative, community, and specialist wound-care services.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Offer patients and carers information and advice about how to recognise a surgical site infection and who to contact if they are concerned. Use an integrated care pathway for healthcare-associated infections to help communicate this information to both patients and all those involved in their care after discharge. [2008]

evidence_statements.population: Adults, young people, and children having a surgical procedure involving a skin incision.
evidence_statements.setting: Perioperative, postoperative, community, and specialist wound-care services.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Always inform patients after their operation if they have been given antibiotics. [2008]
evidence_statements.source_title: Surgical site infections: prevention and treatment — Recommendations

heading_path: Surgical site infections: prevention and treatment
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Always inform patients after their operation if they have been given antibiotics. [2008]

heading_path: Surgical site infections: prevention and treatment

evidence_statements.population: Adults, young people, and children having a surgical procedure involving a skin incision.
evidence_statements.setting: Perioperative, postoperative, community, and specialist wound-care services.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Advise patients to shower or have a bath (or help patients to shower, bath or bed bath) using soap, either the day before, or on the day of, surgery. [2008]
evidence_statements.source_title: Surgical site infections: prevention and treatment — Recommendations

heading_path: Surgical site infections: prevention and treatment

evidence_statements.population: Adults, young people, and children having a surgical procedure involving a skin incision.
evidence_statements.setting: Perioperative, postoperative, community, and specialist wound-care services.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Consider nasal mupirocin in combination with a chlorhexidine body wash before procedures in which Staphylococcus aureus is a likely cause of a surgical site infection. This should be locally determined and take into account: the type of procedure individual patient risk factors the increased risk of side effects in preterm infants (see the recommendation on risks in babies in the section on antiseptic skin preparation ) the potential impact of infection. [2019]
evidence_statements.source_title: Surgical site infections: prevention and treatment — Recommendations

heading_path: Surgical site infections: prevention and treatment

evidence_statements.population: Adults, young people, and children having a surgical procedure involving a skin incision.
evidence_statements.setting: Perioperative, postoperative, community, and specialist wound-care services.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Maintain surveillance on antimicrobial resistance associated with the use of mupirocin. For information on antimicrobial stewardship programmes, see the NICE guideline on antimicrobial stewardship: systems and processes for effective antimicrobial medicine use . [2019] For a short explanation of why the committee made the 2019 recommendations and how they might affect practice, see the rationale and impact section on nasal decolonisation . Full details of the evidence and the committee's discussion are in evidence review A: nasal decontamination in the prevention of surgical site infection .

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Do not use hair removal routinely to reduce the risk of surgical site infection. [2008]

heading_path: Surgical site infections: prevention and treatment

evidence_statements.population: Adults, young people, and children having a surgical procedure involving a skin incision.
evidence_statements.setting: Perioperative, postoperative, community, and specialist wound-care services.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: If hair has to be removed, use electric clippers with a single-use head on the day of surgery. Do not use razors for hair removal, because they increase the risk of surgical site infection. [2008]
evidence_statements.source_title: Surgical site infections: prevention and treatment — Recommendations

evidence_statements.population: Adults, young people, and children having a surgical procedure involving a skin incision.
evidence_statements.setting: Perioperative, postoperative, community, and specialist wound-care services.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Give patients specific theatre wear that is appropriate for the procedure and clinical setting, and that provides easy access to the operative site and areas for placing devices, such as intravenous cannulas. Take into account the patient's comfort and dignity. [2008]
evidence_statements.source_title: Surgical site infections: prevention and treatment — Recommendations

evidence_statements.setting: Perioperative, postoperative, community, and specialist wound-care services.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: All staff should wear specific non-sterile theatre wear in all areas where operations are undertaken. [2008]

evidence_statements.population: Adults, young people, and children having a surgical procedure involving a skin incision.
evidence_statements.setting: Perioperative, postoperative, community, and specialist wound-care services.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Give antibiotic prophylaxis to patients before: clean surgery involving the placement of a prosthesis or implant clean-contaminated surgery contaminated surgery . [2008] For advice on antibiotic prophylaxis before caesarean birth, see the section on timing of antibiotic administration in NICE's guideline on caesarean birth . For information on antimicrobial stewardship programmes see the NICE guideline on antimicrobial stewardship: systems and processes for effective antimicrobial medicine use .

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Use the local antibiotic formulary and always take into account the potential adverse effects when choosing specific antibiotics for prophylaxis. [2008]
evidence_statements.source_title: Surgical site infections: prevention and treatment — Recommendations

heading_path: Surgical site infections: prevention and treatment
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Use the local antibiotic formulary and always take into account the potential adverse effects when choosing specific antibiotics for prophylaxis. [2008]

heading_path: Surgical site infections: prevention and treatment

evidence_statements.population: Adults, young people, and children having a surgical procedure involving a skin incision.
evidence_statements.setting: Perioperative, postoperative, community, and specialist wound-care services.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Consider giving a single dose of antibiotic prophylaxis intravenously on starting anaesthesia. However, give prophylaxis earlier for operations in which a tourniquet is used. [2008]
evidence_statements.source_title: Surgical site infections: prevention and treatment — Recommendations

heading_path: Surgical site infections: prevention and treatment
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Consider giving a single dose of antibiotic prophylaxis intravenously on starting anaesthesia. However, give prophylaxis earlier for operations in which a tourniquet is used. [2008]

heading_path: Surgical site infections: prevention and treatment

evidence_statements.population: Adults, young people, and children having a surgical procedure involving a skin incision.
evidence_statements.setting: Perioperative, postoperative, community, and specialist wound-care services.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Before giving antibiotic prophylaxis, take into account the timing and pharmacokinetics (for example, the serum half-life) and necessary infusion time of the antibiotic. Give a repeat dose of antibiotic prophylaxis when the operation is longer than the half-life of the antibiotic given. [2008]
evidence_statements.source_title: Surgical site infections: prevention and treatment — Recommendations

evidence_statements.population: Adults, young people, and children having a surgical procedure involving a skin incision.
evidence_statements.setting: Perioperative, postoperative, community, and specialist wound-care services.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Give antibiotic treatment (in addition to prophylaxis) to patients having surgery on a dirty or infected wound . [2008]
evidence_statements.source_title: Surgical site infections: prevention and treatment — Recommendations

exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Give antibiotic treatment (in addition to prophylaxis) to patients having surgery on a dirty or infected wound . [2008]

evidence_statements.population: Adults, young people, and children having a surgical procedure involving a skin incision.
evidence_statements.setting: Perioperative, postoperative, community, and specialist wound-care services.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Inform patients before the operation, whenever possible, if they will need antibiotic prophylaxis, and afterwards if they have been given antibiotics during their operation. [2008]
evidence_statements.source_title: Surgical site infections: prevention and treatment — Recommendations

exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Inform patients before the operation, whenever possible, if they will need antibiotic prophylaxis, and afterwards if they have been given antibiotics during their operation. [2008]

evidence_statements.population: Adults, young people, and children having a surgical procedure involving a skin incision.
evidence_statements.setting: Perioperative, postoperative, community, and specialist wound-care services.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: The operating team should wash their hands prior to the first operation on the list using an aqueous antiseptic surgical solution, with a single-use brush or pick for the nails, and ensure that hands and nails are visibly clean. [2008]
evidence_statements.source_title: Surgical site infections: prevention and treatment — Recommendations

evidence_statements.population: Adults, young people, and children having a surgical procedure involving a skin incision.
evidence_statements.setting: Perioperative, postoperative, community, and specialist wound-care services.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Before subsequent operations, hands should be washed using either an alcoholic hand rub or an antiseptic surgical solution. If hands are soiled then they should be washed again with an antiseptic surgical solution. [2008]

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Do not use non-iodophor-impregnated incise drapes routinely for surgery as they may increase the risk of surgical site infection. [2008]

evidence_statements.setting: Perioperative, postoperative, community, and specialist wound-care services.

evidence_statements.population: Adults, young people, and children having a surgical procedure involving a skin incision.
evidence_statements.setting: Perioperative, postoperative, community, and specialist wound-care services.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: When deciding which antiseptic skin preparation to use, options may include those in table 1. [2019] Table 1 Options for antiseptic skin preparation When Choice of antiseptic skin preparation First choice unless contraindicated or the surgical site is next to a mucous membrane Alcohol-based solution of chlorhexidine Alternative if the surgical site is next to a mucous membrane Aqueous solution of chlorhexidine Alternative if chlorhexidine is contraindicated Alcohol-based solution of povidone-iodine If both an alcohol-based solution and chlorhexidine are unsuitable Aqueous solution of povidone-iodine Some products may not be licenced for surgical site antiseptic skin preparation, check individual SPCs and NICE's information on prescribing medicines .
evidence_statements.source_title: Surgical site infections: prevention and treatment — Recommendations

heading_path: Surgical site infections: prevention and treatment

evidence_statements.population: Adults, young people, and children having a surgical procedure involving a skin incision.
evidence_statements.setting: Perioperative, postoperative, community, and specialist wound-care services.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: If diathermy is to be carried out: use evaporation to dry antiseptic skin preparations and avoid pooling of alcohol-based preparations. [2019] For a short explanation of why the committee made the 2019 recommendations and how they might affect practice, see the rationale and impact section on antiseptic skin preparation . Full details of the evidence and the committee's discussion are in evidence review B: skin antiseptics in the prevention of surgical site infection .

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Maintain patient temperature in line with NICE's guideline on hypothermia: prevention and management in adults having surgery . [2008]
evidence_statements.source_title: Surgical site infections: prevention and treatment — Recommendations

heading_path: Surgical site infections: prevention and treatment
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Maintain patient temperature in line with NICE's guideline on hypothermia: prevention and management in adults having surgery . [2008]

heading_path: Surgical site infections: prevention and treatment

evidence_statements.population: Adults, young people, and children having a surgical procedure involving a skin incision.
evidence_statements.setting: Perioperative, postoperative, community, and specialist wound-care services.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Maintain optimal oxygenation during surgery. In particular, give patients sufficient oxygen during major surgery and in the recovery period to ensure that a haemoglobin saturation of more than 95% is maintained. [2008]
evidence_statements.source_title: Surgical site infections: prevention and treatment — Recommendations

heading_path: Surgical site infections: prevention and treatment
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Maintain optimal oxygenation during surgery. In particular, give patients sufficient oxygen during major surgery and in the recovery period to ensure that a haemoglobin saturation of more than 95% is maintained. [2008]

heading_path: Surgical site infections: prevention and treatment

evidence_statements.population: Adults, young people, and children having a surgical procedure involving a skin incision.
evidence_statements.setting: Perioperative, postoperative, community, and specialist wound-care services.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Maintain adequate perfusion during surgery. See additional recommendations on intravenous fluids and cardiac monitoring for adults in NICE's guideline on perioperative care in adults . [2008, amended 2020]
evidence_statements.source_title: Surgical site infections: prevention and treatment — Recommendations

heading_path: Surgical site infections: prevention and treatment
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Maintain adequate perfusion during surgery. See additional recommendations on intravenous fluids and cardiac monitoring for adults in NICE's guideline on perioperative care in adults . [2008, amended 2020]

heading_path: Surgical site infections: prevention and treatment

evidence_statements.population: Adults, young people, and children having a surgical procedure involving a skin incision.
evidence_statements.setting: Perioperative, postoperative, community, and specialist wound-care services.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Do not give insulin routinely to patients who do not have diabetes to optimise blood glucose postoperatively as a means of reducing the risk of surgical site infection. See the additional recommendation on blood glucose control for adults in NICE's guideline on perioperative care in adults . [2008, amended 2020]
evidence_statements.source_title: Surgical site infections: prevention and treatment — Recommendations

evidence_statements.population: Adults, young people, and children having a surgical procedure involving a skin incision.
evidence_statements.setting: Perioperative, postoperative, community, and specialist wound-care services.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: When surgical site infection is suspected by the presence of cellulitis, either by a new infection or an infection caused by treatment failure, give the patient an antibiotic that covers the likely causative organisms. Consider local resistance patterns and the results of microbiological tests in choosing an antibiotic. For information on antimicrobial stewardship programmes see the NICE guideline on antimicrobial stewardship: systems and processes for effective antimicrobial medicine use . [2008]
evidence_statements.source_title: Surgical site infections: prevention and treatment — Recommendations

Antibiotic treatment of surgical site infection and treatment failure

evidence_statements.setting: Perioperative, postoperative, community, and specialist wound-care services.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Use a structured approach to care to improve overall management of surgical wounds. This should include preoperative assessments to identify people with potential wound healing problems. Enhanced education of healthcare workers, patients and carers, and sharing of clinical expertise is needed to support this. [2008]

heading_path: Surgical site infections: prevention and treatment

evidence_statements.population: Adults, young people, and children having a surgical procedure involving a skin incision.
evidence_statements.setting: Perioperative, postoperative, community, and specialist wound-care services.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Occurs when a wound is deliberately left open at the end of an operation because of excessive bacterial contamination, particularly by anaerobes or when there is a risk of devitalised tissue, which leads to infection and delayed healing. It may be sutured within a few days (delayed primary closure), or much later when the wound is clean and granulating (secondary closure), or left to complete healing naturally without suturing.
evidence_statements.source_title: Surgical site infections: prevention and treatment — Recommendations

evidence_statements.population: Adults, young people, and children having a surgical procedure involving a skin incision.
evidence_statements.setting: Perioperative, postoperative, community, and specialist wound-care services.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: A surgical wound with local signs and symptoms of infection, for example, heat, redness, pain and swelling, and (in more serious cases) with systemic signs of fever or a raised white blood cell count. Infection in the surgical wound may prevent healing, causing the wound edges to separate, or it may cause an abscess to form in the deeper tissues. Definitions of the severity of surgical site infections vary and this should be taken into account when comparing reported rates of surgical site infection.
evidence_statements.source_title: Surgical site infections: prevention and treatment — Recommendations

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Clean: an incision in which no inflammation is encountered in a surgical procedure, without a break in sterile technique, and during which the respiratory, alimentary or genitourinary tracts are not entered. Clean-contaminated: an incision through which the respiratory, alimentary, or genitourinary tract is entered under controlled conditions but with no contamination encountered. Contaminated: an incision undertaken during an operation in which there is a major break in sterile technique or gross spillage from the gastrointestinal tract, or an incision in which acute, non-purulent inflammation is encountered. Open traumatic wounds that are more than 12 to 24 hours old also fall into this category.
